coredata.dc:title: Bandgap engineering of nanosized carbon dots through electron-accepting functionalization
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Choi, Jongwan
Kim, Nakjoong
Oh, Jin-Woo
Kim, Felix Sunjoo
coredata.dc:description: We report tuning of energy bandgap of nanosized carbon dots by chemical modification with electron-accepting moieties. The carbon dot derivatives were synthesized by oxidation and hydrothermal reaction, followed by addition/substitution reactions, resulting in uniform-sized carbon dots. Structural characterization and photophysical investigation were performed by applying various microscopic and spectroscopic techniques. The bandgap of the carbon dots was controlled by chemical functionalization with nitro and nitrile groups, as evidenced by the red-shift in the absorption and photoluminescence emission spectra. Calculations based on density functional theory were employed to support the observed bandgap tuning via functionalization of the carbon dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Graphene quantum dot
Photophysical property
Electron acceptor
Bandgap engineering

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic route for synthesis of pristine carbon dot and carbon dot derivatives functionalized with nitro and nitrile groups.
FT-IR spectra of GO, CD, NO2-CD, and CN-CD.

(a) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey scans of carbon dot, NO2-CD, and CN-CD. (b–d) High-resolution C1s spectra of (b) carbon dot, (c) NO2-CD, and (d) CN-CD. (e–f) N1s spectra of (e) NO2-CD and (f) CN-CD.

(a–c) TEM images of (a) carbon dot, (b) NO2-CD, and (c) CN-CD. Scale bar: 5nm. (d–f) High-resolution TEM images (upper) and fast-Fourier-transform (FFT) patterns showing the hexagonal lattice (under) of (d) carbon dot, (e) NO2-CD, and (f) CN-CD. Scale bar: 1nm. (g–i) AFM images with height analysis information for (g) carbon dot, (h) NO2-CD, and (i) CN-CD. Image size: 5μm×5μm.

(a) UV–vis absorption and (b) photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of carbon dot derivatives. Excitation wavelength of PL is 360nm. (c) Photographic image of carbon dot, NO2-CD, and CN-CD under UV irradiation (365nm). (d) Coordinates of carbon dot, NO2-CD, and CN-CD in chromaticity space (CIE1931).

(a) Optimized structures and corresponding energy levels of frontier molecular orbitals of carbon dot, NO2-CD, and CN-CD. (b) Calculated relative band-gap energy of NO2-CD and CN-CD with different number of functional groups. Calculation was done at the PWC/DNP level of theory.

head.ce:title: Bandgap engineering of nanosized carbon dots through electron-accepting functionalization

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report tuning of energy bandgap of nanosized carbon dots by chemical modification with electron-accepting moieties. The carbon dot derivatives were synthesized by oxidation and hydrothermal reaction, followed by addition/substitution reactions, resulting in uniform-sized carbon dots. Structural characterization and photophysical investigation were performed by applying various microscopic and spectroscopic techniques. The bandgap of the carbon dots was controlled by chemical functionalization with nitro and nitrile groups, as evidenced by the red-shift in the absorption and photoluminescence emission spectra. Calculations based on density functional theory were employed to support the observed bandgap tuning via functionalization of the carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:para: Optoelectronically active and fluorescent materials have enabled scientific and engineering breakthroughs in applications of efficient lighting, chemical sensing, and biomedical imaging [1–7]. Various organic and organometallic dyes and inorganic semiconductor quantum dots have shown promising properties with strong luminescence and well-defined/controlled emission. However, these classes of materials generally suffer from many drawbacks in terms of photo-stability, processability, intrinsic toxicity, and environmental risk, limiting their practical applications. Therefore, on-going efforts continue to be expended in the search for new types of fluorescent materials.

Carbon-based nanomaterials have recently emerged as a new class of fluorescent materials due to their photo-stability, water solubility, and potential biocompatibility [8–11]. In particular, nanosized carbon dot and its derivatives have been applied in photocatalysis, optoelectronic devices, and chemical sensors for ion detection with high photoluminescence efficiency [12–21]. Bottom-up and top-down processes have been used to obtain nanosized carbon dots. The bottom-up process utilizes small molecules, such as hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene, glucose, citric acid, and 3-iodo-4-bromoaniline, and the target nanomaterials are synthesized through methods such as stepwise organic synthesis, thermal pyrolysis, and solvothermal treatment [22–25]. The other approach, i.e. the top-down process, involves the cleavage of carbon-based bulk materials into nanometer-sized materials through hydrothermal, electrochemical, and ultrasonic-assisted cutting, nanolithography using a block-copolymer mask, ball-milling, and chemical etching [26–30].

The physical properties and electronic structures of carbon dots (CDs), also known graphene quantum dots (GQDs) or nanosized graphene oxides (NGOs), can be easily altered by using various graphene sources and synthetic methods [31]. Consequently, the absorption and photoluminescence emission spectra can be readily tuned. Recently, a few studies have shown that the bandgap of nanosized graphene dots depends on the size and shape, surface structure, and doping state with heteroatoms [32–40]. Generally, the bandgap of nanosized graphene dots decreases with increasing size. Peng et al. tuned the photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots with various sizes by adjusting the processing parameters [32]. Another approach for controlling the bandgap and photoluminescence emission is doping of graphene with foreign atoms. Li et al. demonstrated a blue-shift in the photoluminescence of nanosized graphene by introduction of nitrogen atoms. This phenomenon is believed to originate from changes in the chemical and electronic characteristics due to the high electron affinity of the nitrogen atoms [38]. Feng et al. also observed a red-shift in the photoluminescence of nanosized graphene doped with highly electronegative fluorine atoms [39]. However, other than size control and elemental doping, effective methods for controlling the bandgap are not readily accessible for nanosized graphene and graphene derivatives.

Chemical functionalization of molecules is a powerful and well-known way to control the photoluminescence and optoelectronic properties. As the structure of a molecule governs its properties, the desired functionality can be easily obtained by molecular design [41,42]. The specific functionalization can therefore be very useful for bio- and chemical-sensing applications, where any false signal must be minimized. Along this line, various chemical modification methods, including electron-rich amino-functionalization, reduction of oxidized groups, and dehydration between quinone and 1,2-diamine, have been developed to engineer nanosized graphene [37,43].

Herein, we report a novel chemical approach for the synthesis of nanosized carbon dot derivatives modified with nitro and nitrile groups, as a means to engineer the bandgap of carbon dots. Molecules with strong electron-accepting groups tend to have a relatively low-lying lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy level. Based on this concept, we rationally designed carbon dot derivatives containing strong electron-withdrawing functional groups, such as NO2 and CN, thereby achieving modified electronic structures, resulting in a narrow bandgap and red-shifted photoluminescence emission. The synthesis involves chemical oxidation and hydrothermal reaction, followed by addition or substitution reactions. Based on microscopic imaging, the carbon dot derivatives have uniform dimensions of 3.4nm in diameter and 1.8nm in thickness. The effects of functionalization of carbon dots on the optical properties are studied by using various spectroscopic methods. Quantum chemical calculation is also used to support the observed photophysical properties of the functionalized carbon dots.

Nanosized carbon dot (CD; 2) was prepared by oxidizing graphite through a modified Hummer’s method [44], followed by hydrothermal reaction. In brief, 0.5g of pyrolytic graphite and 0.05g of sodium nitrate were added in 40mL of concentrated sulfuric acid. Potassium permanganate (3.0g) was slowly added to the mixture with vigorous stirring in an ice bath. The reaction temperature was then raised to 35°C and stirring was continued for 1h. Deionized water (100mL) was slowly added to the reaction mixture. The solution was kept at 95°C for an additional 1h. The brownish solution was treated by adding 5.0mL of hydrogen peroxide in order to convert the permanganate ions and manganese dioxide into soluble manganese sulfate. The product was isolated from the solution by centrifugation and filtration with deionized water, and the graphene oxide (GO; 1) was again dispersed in deionized water. The pH was adjusted to 8 by adding sodium hydroxide. For the hydrothermal treatment, the solution was heated at 200°C for 12h in a Teflon-lined autoclave. After cooling to room temperature, the reaction mixture was filtered through an anodized aluminum oxide filter (0.02-μm pore) to remove the large graphene flakes. The resulting carbon dot was further purified through dialysis tubing (molecular-weight cut-off: 1000Da) in deionized water for 3days to remove residual ions.

Nitro-functionalized carbon dot (NO2-CD; 3) was synthesized by dropwise addition of fuming nitric acid into a dispersion of CD (2) in acetic acid in an ice bath. The mixture was stirred at 0°C for 8h. The reaction mixture was poured into ice-water and neutralized with a saturated solution of sodium bicarbonate. The NO2-CD was then purified through dialysis tubing for 3 days.

Nitrile-functionalized carbon dot (CN-CD; 5) was prepared in a three-step procedure. Carbon dot (0.072g) was firstly dispersed in 3mL of oxalyl chloride under N2 atmosphere. After adding dry N,N-dimethylformamide (10μL) to the solution, the mixture was stirred for 24h at 60°C with reflux under N2 atmosphere. The acyl-chloride-functionalized carbon dot (4) was obtained after removing residual oxalyl chloride via evaporation. The product was re-dispersed in dry acetonitrile, and copper cyanide (CuCN) and dry sodium iodide were added to the solution under a N2 purge. The reaction mixture was stirred for 24h at room temperature. After solvent evaporation, the acyl-cyanide-functionalized carbon dot was purified by column chromatography (silica gel; toluene:ethyl acetate=2:1). CN-CD (5) was synthesized from acyl-cyanide-functionalized carbon dot (0.035g) re-dispersed in toluene. Tetrakis(triphenylphosphine)palladium(0) (Pd(PPh3)4; 0.020g) and cesium fluoride (CsF; 0.060g) were added to the solution. The reaction mixture was stirred for 24h with reflux. CN-CD was then obtained by solvent evaporation and purification through column chromatography (silica gel; toluene:ethylacetate=4:1).

The chemical structure of carbon dot and its functionalization were confirmed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR; ABB Bomem Mb 100; KBr pellets) analysis at a resolution of 4cm−1 and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Thermo Scientific Co.) with a monochromated Al-Kα source. Absorption and fluorescence spectra were obtained from aqueous solutions by using an Optizen Pro POP instrument and an ISS spectrometer, respectively. Microscopic imaging of the drop-cast samples was performed by using a transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM; JEOL JEM 2100F) and an atomic force microscope in the AC mode (AFM; Park System NX-10).

Geometry optimization and calculation of the molecular orbital electron density and the corresponding energy levels of nanosized carbon dot and the functionalized carbon dots were performed with density functional theory (DFT) using the Dmol3 program in the Materials Studio 4.1.2 packages (Accelrys Inc.) [45,46]. The calculation was performed by using the local-density approximation (LDA) with the Perdew Wang Correlation (PWC) functional [47]. The double numerical plus polarization (DNP) basis set, for which the convergence tolerance was set to 10−5 Ha for the energy, 2×10−3 Ha/Å for the force, and 10−6 Ha for the electronic self-consistent field (SCF) tolerance, was used.

We prepared nanosized carbon dot and its functionalized derivatives by the introduction of electron-accepting nitrile (CN) or nitro (NO2) groups in order to control the bandgap energy and frontier molecular orbital energy levels. The synthesis and functionalization of carbon dots are summarized in Scheme 1 . Briefly, graphene oxide (GO; 1) was prepared by a modified Hummer’s method [44]. The additional oxidation of GO facilitates easy reaction and funsctionalization. Nanosized carbon dot (2) was synthesized through cleavage of the processed GO via hydrothermal reaction. Two different routes were used to attach the functional groups to carbon dots. Nitro-functionalized carbon dot (NO2-CD; 3) was obtained via a one-step addition reaction by using fuming nitric acid in the presence of an acid catalyst. GO, carbon dot, and NO2-CD are all readily processable in water. In the case of nitrile functionalization to generate CN-CD (5), a substitution reaction mechanism was applied in three steps, involving sequential formation of acyl chloride [48], acyl cyanide [49], and nitrile groups [50]. The resulting CN-CD was only slightly soluble in water because most of the carboxylic acid groups were replaced by nitrile groups.

In order to confirm the chemical structures of the pristine and functionalized carbon dots, the samples were characterized via FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 1 , the spectra of as-prepared GO, carbon dot, and the series of CD derivatives showed a broad absorption of aromatic CC bonds and other sp2 carbon bonds at 1650–1600cm−1 with CO bond at near 1140cm−1, suggesting the presence of graphene oxide-like backbones. Hydroxyl groups from the alcohol and carboxylic acid groups on the oxides were indicated by the broad band in the range of 3700–3000cm−1, along with a band corresponding to the bending vibration of the alcohols at 1380cm−1. The small stretching peaks at 2720 and 2820cm−1 in the FTIR profile of the carbon dots suggest the formation of a fraction of aldehydes as a result of partial reduction of the carboxylic acids by hydrothermal reaction.

In the case of NO2-CD, a distinct shoulder was observed at 1470cm−1, attributed to the nitro groups. It is also noticeable that the peak at 1350cm−1, overlapping with peaks of the carbonyl groups, was enhanced in the case of NO2-CD. Functionalization with the nitrile groups could be more easily confirmed. A new peak corresponding to the nitrile groups was observed at 2240cm−1 in the spectrum of CN-CD, confirming successful nitrile functionalization after the three-step synthesis procedure. It was also expected that most of the carboxylic acid groups would be replaced during functionalization in the case of CN-CD, as confirmed by the absence of the characteristic peaks of carboxylic acids near 1300cm−1 and weakening of the CO stretching vibration shoulder at 1700cm−1 compared to that in the profile of pristine carbon dot.

The elemental contents and nature of the chemical bonds were also identified by XPS (Fig. 2 ). The XPS survey scans showed two peaks at 284eV and 531eV corresponding to C 1s and O 1s in pristine carbon dot, respectively. After functionalization with nitrogen-containing groups, the profiles of NO2-CD and CN-CD showed an additional peak at 401eV in the N 1s region, along with the C 1s and O 1s peaks. The high-resolution XPS spectra revealed the detailed bonding nature of the elements. The high-resolution C 1s peaks of all the carbon dots could be deconvoluted into at least three peaks with binding energies of 284.5 (sp2 carbon), 286.1 (CO), and 288.2eV (CO). There were also small π–π* satellite peaks near 291.5eV from the aromatic rings. The spectrum of NO2-CD showed an additional peak at 285.2eV, which corresponds to a CN single bond. The peak s corresponding to sp2 carbon, CO single bond, and CO double bond were decreased along the evolution of a new peak corresponding to CN single bond after the addition reaction. In case of CN-CD, the XPS profile showed a new peak at 286.7eV, corresponding to a CN triple bond. Two peaks corresponding to sp2 carbon and CO bond were almost unchanged after functionalization with nitrile groups, meaning that the substitution reaction does not directly affect CC and CO bonds. The peak corresponding to CN triple bond increased as the one for CO double bond decreased. The nitrogen 1s profile of the functionalized NO2-CD showed signals at 398.9 (CN), and 403.5 and 406.1eV (for both nitro groups). The peaks of CN-CD were observed at 399.5eV and 397.0eV, both corresponding to CN. The XPS data complement the FT-IR spectroscopic data, confirming formation of the targeted structures of carbon dot and the carbon dot derivatives.

Fig. 3 shows the morphological features of the series of carbon dots. As shown in the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, after extensive oxidation followed by hydrothermal reaction, all the carbon dots had a quasi-spherical shape. Size exclusion filtration allowed the separation of particles with a mean radius of 3.3–3.4nm. The size of the carbon dots could be easily controlled by adjusting the reaction time and pore size of the filters. The diameters of carbon dot and the functionalized carbon dots were essentially identical, suggesting that the size of the carbon dots is not affected by the functionalization process.

The high-resolution TEM images and the corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) patterns reveal the detailed lattice structures of the hexagonal carbon network of the carbon dots (Fig. 3d–f). The spacing distances determined from the images were 2.41Å for pristine carbon dot, 2.38Å for NO2-CD, and 2.37Å for CN-CD, which are in good agreement with the parameters of pristine graphene (∼2.4Å) [51]. These results indicate that the carbon dots are composed of well-structured graphene cores. The thickness of the pristine carbon dot and carbon dot derivatives were analyzed by using atomic force microscopy (AFM) imaging (Fig. 3g–i). The height of the carbon dots was ∼1.8nm, suggesting that most of the carbon dots consist of 2–3 layers of graphene nanosheets. These few-layer graphene species are commonly observed in chemically exfoliated graphene oxides.

The degree of bandgap tuning resulting from carbon dot functionalization was investigated by UV/vis absorption and photoluminescence spectroscopy, combined with computational study. Experimentally, spectroscopic investigation was performed on aqueous solutions of the synthesized carbon dots, as shown in Fig. 4 . The pristine carbon dot absorbed most strongly below 400nm. The absorption tails shifted toward the longer wavelength region in the case of NO2-CD and CN-CD, which were functionalized with electron-accepting groups. The shift was more prominent for CN-CD, suggesting greater narrowing of the energy gap.

Fig. 4b shows the photoluminescence spectra of pristine carbon dot and the functionalized carbon dots in water under UV excitation at 360nm. The solution of pristine carbon dot emitted visible blue luminescence with a maximum peak at 462nm. Nitro-functionalized carbon dot had a red-shifted emission band at 480nm. CN-CD exhibited an even more red-shifted green emission with a peak at 494nm. Because the size of the carbon dots is more or less uniform throughout the series of carbon dots, the red-shifts of the emission color indicate changes in the electronic structures of the carbon dots and bandgap narrowing caused by functionalization of the carbon dots with electron-accepting nitro and nitrile groups. The shift of photoluminescence of the functionalized carbon dots showed a similar tendency observed in conjugated molecules and organometallic compound as previously reported [52,53]. The photographic images of the carbon dot solutions under UV irradiation (wavelength=365nm) represent the tunable emission of carbon dot and functionalized carbon dots (Fig. 4c). In x–y chromaticity space (CIE 1931), carbon dot, NO2-CD, and CN-CD had coordinates of (0.157, 0.133), (0.176, 0.235), and (0.224, 0.367), respectively.

Quantum chemical calculations based on density function theory (DFT) support the observations made in the photophysical study. Geometrical optimization and the energy level calculation for the frontier molecular orbitals of carbon dot and functionalized carbon dots were performed using the Dmol3 program in the Materials Studio 4.1.2 packages with the local-density approximation at the PWC/DNP level of theory (Fig. 5 ) [45–47]. We have designed a model carbon bot consisting of 37 six-membered carbon rings for the simulation. Because our carbon dots derived from graphene oxide, the edge of the carbon dot was decorated with multiple carboxylic acid and hydroxyl groups. In the case of NO2-CD, nitro groups were added to the model carbon dot. The bandgap decreased from 1.12eV for the model carbon dot to 1.05eV for NO2-CD. In the case of CN-CD, a large portion of the carboxylic acids in the pristine carbon dot was replaced by nitrile groups. Such substitution of carboxylic acids by nitriles also resulted in the smaller bandgap of 1.03eV for CN-CD. That is, the calculation suggests that for the same number of substitutions, the nitrile groups have a stronger bandgap narrowing effect than the nitro groups. The results are in agreement with the absorption and emission spectroscopic analyses.

Unlike a molecule with a well-defined structure, a carbon dot has a number of activation sites highly reactive with other substances by the lots of oxygen containing groups, and many substitution reactions on one of nanosized carbon dot may occur simultaneously. Therefore, multiple substituted or additional reactions may occur rather than one or two reactions during functionalization. Regarding this point, we performed a DFT calculation to investigate the effect of the number of electron-withdrawing groups for carbon dot. As shown in Fig. 5b, the bandgap became narrower with an increase in the number of substituted electron-withdrawing groups on the model carbon dots. The substitution on one site resulted in a reduction of the bandgap by roughly 0.01–0.02eV. The tendency is similar for both electron-withdrawing groups, although the nitrile has a stronger effect than nitro groups on the bandgap reduction. This study demonstrates that the bandgap energy of nanosized graphene can be controlled not only by adjusting its size, but also by varying the attached functional groups through changes in the electronic structure. Moreover, to precisely tune the bandgap of the carbon dots, the degree of functionalization and the functionalizing species must be judiciously selected.

The bandgap of nanosized carbon dots was successfully tuned via chemical functionalization of graphene oxides with electron-accepting nitro and nitrile groups. Various spectroscopic methods confirmed the synthesis and functionalization of the carbon dots. Morphological characterization revealed the uniform sizes of pristine carbon dot and the carbon dot derivatives with an average diameter of 3.4nm and a thickness of 1.8nm. Functionalization shifted the absorption and photoluminescence spectral bands to longer wavelength regions, indicating bandgap narrowing of the carbon dots. The observed changes in the bandgaps after functionalization also matched the expected trends according to the calculation based on the density functional theory. This study defines the relationship between functionalization with electron-accepting groups and the bandgap energy of the carbon dots. Further optimization of the functional groups should be valuable for diverse applications in electronics and luminescence tagging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grants funded by the Korean Government (MSIP, MOE) (NRF-2017R1C1B5014870, 2017R1D1A1B03029675, 2016R1C1B2014423, and 2016R1A6A1A03013422).
